caveats: a: BLS combines residential, commercial, and community-association managers rather than measuring 531312 alone.
a: IREM measures perceived proptech opportunities, not wage-weighted task exposure or realized AI labor savings.
a: CBRE is far larger and more integrated than a lower-middle-market property manager.
rho: IREM respondents reported cost, integration, and training barriers but did not report five-year AI realization rates.
rho: CBRE's technology resources and integration capabilities may exceed those of target firms.
rho: AI adoption can improve service quality without reducing labor or avoiding future hiring.
e: The frozen firm count uses a 3.96% broad real-estate-operations margin rather than observed property-manager EBITDA.
e: Large integrated firms mix property management with facilities, project, leasing, and advisory services.
e: Contracts may be terminable on sale of a managed property or change of control, weakening practical eligibility.

s5: The survey includes internal and family transfers that may not qualify for the screen.
s5: Property-management mandates may terminate or be rebid when properties or managers change hands.
q: CBRE's global contract mix may not represent independent lower-middle-market managers.
q: The filing documents billing structures but not savings-sharing outcomes after automation.
q: A percentage-of-rent fee can preserve pricing while exposing revenue to occupancy and rent-collection cycles.
d5: BLS employment projections combine residential, commercial, and community-association work and are not service-quantity forecasts.
d5: CBRE is global, very large, and acquisitive; its revenue growth includes mix, price, scope, and acquisitions.
d5: Office distress, property sales, insourcing, and rent-collection weakness can reduce management-fee demand even when physical space persists.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Commercial property owners and investors pay recurring monthly fees based on a fixed amount or a percentage of rental income or receipts; contracts may also reimburse property-dedicated administrative and payroll costs and may include separately priced leasing, engineering, or project services.
lens.excluded_activities: Residential property management, property ownership and leasing for the firm's own account, brokerage-only and transaction-only work, facilities or project management without recurring property-management responsibility, captive owner departments, shell entities, non-control financing situations, and firms outside the target normalized-EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market firms that repeatedly manage office, industrial, retail, and other nonresidential real estate for external owners and investors, including lease administration, property accounting, tenant coordination, vendor oversight, inspections, maintenance coordination, and owner reporting.

caveats: BLS combines residential, commercial, and community-association managers rather than measuring 531312 alone.
IREM measures perceived proptech opportunities, not wage-weighted task exposure or realized AI labor savings.
CBRE is far larger and more integrated than a lower-middle-market property manager.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task and time study from independent nonresidential property-management firms, split across accounting, lease administration, tenant service, engineering coordination, field inspection, vendor management, and senior management, with current automation identified, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: Lease abstraction, invoice and rent-roll processing, routine owner and tenant communications, work-order classification, vendor document review, budgeting support, reporting, and record maintenance are exposed to AI assistance or avoidable hiring. Physical inspections, emergency response, contractor direction, negotiations, relationship management, and accountable legal or operational decisions remain substantially human. Existing property software already automates vacancy posting, maintenance routing, and other routine work, limiting the incremental share.

d5.bridge: Source population/quantity: BLS projects 4% employment growth from 2024 to 2034 for the broader property-manager occupation, and CBRE reported 2025 property-management revenue of $2.579 billion versus $1.976 billion in 2024. Transformation: translated the slow occupational growth and strong but acquisition- and price-affected public-company revenue into a moderated 3% five-year real quantity base for U.S. LMM nonresidential management, with a wide cyclical range. Error direction: employment may understate quantity growth when automation raises productivity, while CBRE revenue growth likely overstates organic LMM real demand because it includes price, scope, mix, geography, and acquisitions.
caveats: BLS employment projections combine residential, commercial, and community-association work and are not service-quantity forecasts.
CBRE is global, very large, and acquisitive; its revenue growth includes mix, price, scope, and acquisitions.
Office distress, property sales, insourcing, and rent-collection weakness can reduce management-fee demand even when physical space persists.
d5.change_evidence: A constant-price five-year series for 531312 management-fee revenue, square footage or properties under third-party management, new mandates, losses, and contract scope, separated from reimbursed costs, acquisitions, and pricing, would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: BLS projects 4% ten-year employment growth for the broader property-manager occupation, while CBRE classifies property management as resilient and reported property-management revenue of $2.579 billion in 2025 versus $1.976 billion in 2024. A moderated five-year real service-quantity range allows for commercial occupancy and rent pressure, outsourcing growth, acquisitions and pricing embedded in public-company revenue, and differences between nonresidential and broader occupational demand.

caveats: The frozen firm count uses a 3.96% broad real-estate-operations margin rather than observed property-manager EBITDA.
Large integrated firms mix property management with facilities, project, leasing, and advisory services.
Contracts may be terminable on sale of a managed property or change of control, weakening practical eligibility.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of 531312 companies with normalized EBITDA, revenue mix, recurring management-fee revenue, reimbursed payroll, client and property concentration, contract term and termination rights, ownership, and transferability would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: The official industry definition is already limited to managing nonresidential real estate for others, and commercial property management is ordinarily contract-based and recurring. Eligibility is reduced by firms whose apparent economics are dominated by brokerage, project work, reimbursed personnel, owner dependence, concentrated mandates, weak contract assignability, or EBITDA outside the target band despite the margin bridge.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 531312 ($16,957,444,118) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 531312 ($44,188,563,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0396 (margins.json[53]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Real Estate (Operations & Services) EBITDA/Sales 3.96% (agency/brokerage/property-management economics, dominant in firm counts)); revenue/employee $269,488 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 531312); class <5: 10,630 firms, avg revenue/firm $447,046 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $17,703 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 2,365 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,287,092 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $50,969 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,323 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,445,732 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $96,851 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,173 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,670,320 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $264,145 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 299 firms, avg revenue/firm $18,330,284 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $725,879 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 124 firms, avg revenue/firm $160,094,766 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,339,753 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 124

o.bridge: Source population/quantity: BLS documents recurring financial, lease, inspection, repair, contractor, complaint, and reporting duties; IREM reports automation of repetitive tasks as the leading proptech-solvable problem; CBRE describes contract-based engineering, lease administration, accounting, reporting, and flexible-workplace services. Transformation: classified routine information handling as increasingly self-serviceable while retaining field coordination, client accountability, compliance, vendor control, and emergency response as operator-required. Error direction: the task evidence may overstate operator retention if owners successfully insource integrated software, while the physical and accountability requirements may make the low case conservative.
caveats: Continued need for property operations does not guarantee continued use of an independent manager.
The BLS task list includes residential and community-association work as well as commercial management.
Integrated owner platforms may unbundle administrative tasks while retaining only field or specialist services.
o.change_evidence: Cohort data tracking commercial owners that replace third-party managers with owner-operated software, including retained scope, staffing, property performance, incident handling, and subsequent re-outsourcing, would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Software and AI can self-serve vacancy posting, communications, records, reporting, invoice processing, and work-order assignment, but commercial properties still require accountable coordination of tenants, vendors, leases, budgets, compliance, inspections, engineering, maintenance, and emergencies. Some owners may insource or buy software directly, yet most of the current full-service management basket remains operator-required.

caveats: CBRE's global contract mix may not represent independent lower-middle-market managers.
The filing documents billing structures but not savings-sharing outcomes after automation.
A percentage-of-rent fee can preserve pricing while exposing revenue to occupancy and rent-collection cycles.
q.change_evidence: Matched commercial property-management contracts and contribution margins before and after automation, including fixed versus rent-linked fees, reimbursed labor, renewal repricing, service levels, client churn, and retained headcount savings, would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Fixed monthly and percentage-of-rent management fees do not mechanically fall when an operator improves back-office productivity, supporting some retention. However, property-dedicated administrative and payroll costs are often reimbursed, so savings in those categories flow directly to owners; contract renewals, competitive rebids, fee pressure, service expansion, and owner demands for transparency share additional benefits.

caveats: IREM respondents reported cost, integration, and training barriers but did not report five-year AI realization rates.
CBRE's technology resources and integration capabilities may exceed those of target firms.
AI adoption can improve service quality without reducing labor or avoiding future hiring.
rho.change_evidence: Audited before-and-after deployments at independent commercial property managers measuring labor hours, response times, accounting errors, work-order resolution, tenant satisfaction, client retention, and implementation cost by workflow would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Digital leases, accounting records, tenant requests, work orders, invoices, and property data make several exposed workflows implementable within five years, and a major operator reports investing in AI and machine learning. Realization is constrained by fragmented owner systems, legacy integrations, data permissions, cybersecurity, training, cost-benefit uncertainty, field adoption, client approval, and the need for human escalation in emergencies and disputes.

s5.bridge: Source population/quantity: Gallup's fall-2024 survey reports that 22% of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell, transfer, or take public within five years. Transformation: treated 22% as an upper anchor and discounted to a 14% base for noncompletion, internal or family transfers, owner-dependent relationships, client consent and termination rights, property turnover, and nonqualifying team or asset transactions. Error direction: broad intentions likely overstate completed qualifying control sales, while consolidation among commercial real-estate service providers could make the low case conservative.
caveats: Gallup covers all U.S. employer-business owners and intentions rather than completed property-management control transfers.
The survey includes internal and family transfers that may not qualify for the screen.
Property-management mandates may terminate or be rebid when properties or managers change hands.
s5.change_evidence: Closed control-sale counts and eligible-firm denominators from property-management M&A advisers, business brokers, contract assignment records, and transaction databases over a five-year cohort would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Employer-business owners report meaningful five-year sale or transfer intentions, and recurring management contracts, staff, systems, and client relationships can support a control transfer. Qualifying completion is discounted for noncompletion, family or employee transfers, owner relationships, client consent or termination rights, property-sale churn, and firms that are absorbed through asset or team transactions rather than a qualifying company sale.

principal_driver: The principal driver is the combination of recurring commercial property-management mandates and automatable lease, accounting, reporting, communication, and work-order workflows.
principal_weakness: The principal weakness is that many labor costs are reimbursed or operationally essential, while commercial real-estate cycles and client rebids can pass savings through or erode mandates.
